Chapter 4: Customer and Feeder Clustering

Chapter 2 plotted a handful of AusNet’s 342 real households side by side and made one plain observation: no two profiles agree. Some households barely draw half a kilowatt all day. Others spike past five kilowatts every evening. That chart was true, and also incomplete. A Distribution System Operator (DSO) cannot plan a feeder, price a tariff, or run a demand-response program around 342 individual shapes. It needs a smaller number of real, recurring patterns underneath that mess, not an average that describes no one.

This chapter builds those patterns from AusNet’s own year of real smart-meter data. It checks whether a customer’s pattern is a stable trait or a one-day snapshot, a question none of this chapter’s own reference papers ask. And it tests whether knowing a customer’s pattern actually predicts anything a DSO would care about: which real Distributed Energy Resources (DER) constraint, voltage or thermal, that customer’s own future Photovoltaic (PV) or Electric Vehicle (EV) adoption is likely to trigger.

Why cluster, not classify

There is no ground-truth “true customer type” label for real households at this scale. No utility keeps one. That makes this a clustering problem, an unsupervised search for structure, not a classification problem with a known answer to check against. Chapter 3’s phase- identification work had a real answer to check its own clustering against. This chapter does not, and leans on validity indices and honest sensitivity checks instead.

The second choice that matters is what “similar” means for a load shape. A small apartment and a large house can have very different absolute demand and still share the same underlying rhythm, both peaking in the evening, both quiet at 3 a.m. Clustering on raw magnitude would separate them by size instead of by behavior. Every profile in this chapter is normalized by its own peak before clustering, the same “shape, not magnitude” principle Chapter 3 used for a different reason, voltage correlation rather than raw voltage tracked phase membership there. Here it is what lets a small household and a large one land in the same archetype when their rhythm actually matches.

This is not new ground. McLoughlin, Duffy, and Conlon compared k-means, k-medoids, and self-organizing maps for exactly this problem, domestic load-shape characterization, and settled the same habit this chapter follows below: check more than one validity index, since no single one reliably picks the right number of clusters alone [1]. Kwac, Flora, and Rajagopal built an encoded load-shape dictionary from real California smart-meter data to drive five program-relevant lifestyle segments, an early demonstration that shape- based clusters map onto real customer behavior, not just statistical artifacts [2]. Haben, Singleton, and Grindrod clustered real UK residential demand directly on behavioral shape, the same instinct this chapter applies to AusNet’s own data [3].

More recently, Rajabi et al. benchmarked clustering techniques for load- pattern segmentation directly, the role this chapter’s own validity-index comparison plays for AusNet specifically [4]. Michalakopoulos et al. ran nearly 5,000 real London households through k-means, k-medoids, hierarchical, and DBSCAN clustering, adding explainable AI so the resulting archetypes are interpretable, not just numerically distinct [5]. Yerbury et al. pushed further still with CROCS, a two-stage framework that first summarizes each real consumer’s own representative daily behaviors, then clusters consumers by how similar those behavior sets are, tested on real Australian smart-meter data, the most current entry in this lineage [6]. None of the five, though, ask whether a customer’s archetype holds up across a full year rather than the one window each one clusters on, the gap this chapter’s own stability check fills.

Key Concept

Shape, not magnitude, is the clustering feature. Two households can draw very different absolute power and still share the same underlying rhythm. A method that clusters on raw magnitude answers “which customers use a similar amount of power,” a question dominated by household size. A method that clusters on peak-normalized shape answers “which customers’ demand rises and falls together,” the question that actually separates behavioral archetypes. Every result in this chapter clusters on shape.

Getting the data

This chapter reuses the same real AusNet smart-meter pool Chapters 1 through 3 already vendored, no new fetch step needed:

uv run python scripts/fetch_part4_ausnet_data.py

Later in this chapter, a real generalization check against NREL’s public ResStock and ComStock building-stock data needs one further fetch, a stratified sample of 760 real US buildings across 19 real subtypes, complementing AusNet’s own real data, not replacing it:

uv run python scripts/fetch_part5_nrel_buildstock_data.py

A real season, not one day

A single day is a noisy read on anyone’s routine: a sick day, a weekend, a one-off late night. Averaging a real customer’s own readings across a season smooths that out while staying specific to that customer, not a population average.

load_data: (342, 365, 48) (customers, days, half-hours)
clustering input: (342, 48) (customers, half-hour steps)

A baseline worth checking, not assuming

The first step is k-means. Rather than assuming the number of clusters, this chapter checks it with three validity indices together: the elbow method on inertia, the silhouette coefficient, and the Davies-Bouldin index, the same combination McLoughlin et al. and Rajabi et al. recommend checking together, since no single index reliably picks the right k alone [1], [4].

k inertia silhouette davies_bouldin
2 312.172 0.227 1.572
3 265.8 0.19 1.609
4 240.341 0.17 1.609
5 220.8 0.163 1.649
6 207.773 0.16 1.698
7 196.869 0.136 1.725
8 188.846 0.143 1.652
9 181.241 0.136 1.69

The small-multiples view above puts each index on its own scale. Normalizing all three to a common 0-1 range and overlaying them makes the same trends easier to compare directly: does the elbow in inertia line up with the silhouette peak, or point somewhere else entirely.

The data show no clear elbow, and the silhouette coefficient peaks at k=2 (0.227) then decays smoothly. Real residential behavior does not split into a small number of cleanly separated groups, a genuine finding about this data, not a modeling failure. k=5 is used from here on, past the strict silhouette optimum (0.163 at k=5), trading a small amount of statistical cleanliness for archetypes specific enough to tell a real story with, a defensible, common choice in applied clustering work, made transparently rather than silently.

Label Count
0 64
1 104
2 74
3 50
4 50

Five visually distinct rhythms: a sharp early-evening peaker, a gradual afternoon-into-evening riser, a late-evening peaker with a small morning bump, and two broadly similar late-night risers, exactly the pair the modest silhouette score was warning about. The chart makes concrete what the number only hinted at: some of these archetypes genuinely overlap.

How much does that overlap actually matter?

“Some overlap” is a qualitative read on a chart. Turning it into a number a DSO could actually use borrows a tool Chapter 3 already built: split conformal prediction. There, it checked phase assignments against a known answer, flagging which customers’ labels deserved a second look. This chapter has no known answer to check against, but the same tool still works, aimed at a different question: not “is this label correct,” but “how many archetypes could this customer plausibly belong to.”

The method fits k-means on part of the customers and holds the rest back. For each held-back customer, it measures the distance from that customer’s profile to its own nearest cluster centroid, a real, checkable number for every customer, not just the ones near a boundary. The held- back customers’ distances pick a threshold, the value 90% of them fall under. Any customer, old or new, then gets a prediction set, not a single label: every archetype whose centroid sits within that same threshold.

Set size Customers
0 20
1 90
2 105
3 83
4 44

Calibrated on 103 held-back customers, the threshold sits at 1.158. Only 90 of 342 customers (26%) get an unambiguous, single-archetype set. Most get two, three, or even four plausible archetypes. Twenty customers (6%) get an empty set: their own best-matching archetype still sits farther from its centroid than 90% of the held-back customers ever did, real households whose behavior does not confidently match any of the five archetypes.

Key Concept

This is the same split conformal machinery Chapter 3 used, aimed at a different job. There, it flagged phase assignments worth a second look against a known answer. Here, with no known answer to check against, it still earns its keep: it turns “some of these archetypes overlap,” a read on a chart, into a checkable, per-customer confidence set. A DSO deciding how much to trust an archetype-based connection or tariff decision now has a real number to weigh that decision against.

Does a more advanced method earn its complexity?

IDEC, Improved Deep Embedded Clustering, pretrains an autoencoder, then jointly optimizes a reconstruction loss and a clustering loss, in principle learning a representation shaped for clustering rather than clustering whatever k-means is handed [7]. Kumar and Mallipeddi apply the same idea to load-pattern segmentation directly, confirming that lineage is still active [8]. Worth testing on this exact data before assuming it helps.

k kmeans_silhouette kmeans_davies_bouldin idec_silhouette idec_davies_bouldin
2 0.227 1.572 0.217 1.609
3 0.19 1.609 0.132 2.079
4 0.17 1.609 0.078 2.687
5 0.163 1.649 0.059 3.132
6 0.16 1.698 0.025 3.817
7 0.136 1.725 0.025 3.829
8 0.143 1.652 0.013 4.554

Common Mistake

IDEC underperforms plain k-means at every k tested, and the gap widens as k grows. This is not a bug. It is what happens when a deep method meets data too small and low-dimensional to need it: 342 customers, 48 features, is not enough for an autoencoder’s extra representational flexibility to earn its own complexity over a much simpler method already finding the same structure. The lesson is not “IDEC is bad.” It is that a more advanced method has to prove itself on the data actually in hand, not get credited for sophistication it does not need here.

Are these archetypes stable, or a snapshot artifact?

None of this chapter’s ten reference papers test this directly. Every one clusters on a single representative period and treats the result as fixed. AusNet’s own real data is a full year, 365 days per customer, and most of it goes unused once one season is picked. Re-clustering on different quarters and comparing the result answers a question none of the ten papers do: is a customer’s archetype a stable trait, or does it drift?

Checking stability needs a metric first. The Adjusted Rand Index (Adjusted Rand Index (ARI)) compares two sets of cluster labels for the same customers. For every pair of customers, it asks whether both label sets agree on putting them together or keeping them apart. A score of 1.0 means the two labelings agree on every pair. A score of 0.0 means the agreement is no better than what random labels would give by chance, since ARI corrects for that chance level. There is no ground truth to compare against here, so the two labelings being compared are two independent, real clusterings of the same 342 customers, one built from each quarter’s own data. If a customer’s archetype is a stable trait, the quarter-to-quarter clusterings should agree closely. If archetypes actually drift over the year, they should not.

quarter silhouette
Q1 0.163
Q2 0.159
Q3 0.15
Q4 0.137

Every quarter clusters about as cleanly as the season used above, so whatever comes next is not an artifact of picking an unusually messy period. The real test is whether the same customer lands in a matching archetype across quarters, not just whether each quarter clusters cleanly on its own.

pair ari
Q1 vs Q2 0.128
Q1 vs Q3 0.062
Q1 vs Q4 0.144
Q2 vs Q3 0.336
Q2 vs Q4 0.158
Q3 vs Q4 0.154

The six pairwise ARI scores are a summary. The transition matrix below shows exactly where the disagreement comes from: which archetype each customer moves to between the first and the last quarter of the year.

The ARI scores are low, between 0.06 and 0.34. The heatmap shows why: there is no strong diagonal. If archetypes were a fixed trait, most customers would keep the same label from Q1 to Q4, filling the diagonal cells. Instead the counts spread across the grid.

Before treating this as real drift, one thing is worth checking first: how much do the labels move around on their own, just from re-running k-means on the same quarter with a different random seed?

pair ari
seed0 vs seed1 0.976
seed0 vs seed2 0.967
seed0 vs seed3 0.967
seed0 vs seed4 0.978
seed1 vs seed2 0.979
seed1 vs seed3 0.991
seed1 vs seed4 0.968
seed2 vs seed3 0.97
seed2 vs seed4 0.989
seed3 vs seed4 0.959

Key Concept

Same-quarter, different-seed agreement sits at 0.96-0.99. K-means is highly deterministic on this data, ruling out algorithmic noise as the explanation. Cross-quarter agreement is 0.06-0.34, an order of magnitude lower, showing two clearly separated clusters of points in the chart above. The gap between these numbers is the actual finding: this is not the clustering method being unreliable. Real customers genuinely change which archetype they belong to as the year moves on. This is not a stable trait a utility can safely assume holds from one assessment to the next.

Can archetype really predict DER risk?

In Chapter 1, three ways DER strains a feeder were identified: fluctuation, load growth, and constraint violations. Chapter 2 showed that PV and EV adoption bind on different constraints on this same feeder, voltage first for PV, thermal first for EV. If archetype membership predicts which constraint a customer is likely to trigger, network planning can shift from reactive, a hosting-capacity study run after a connection request arrives, to proactive, knowing the risk before it does.

This is checkable directly. Chapter 2’s own run_penetration() sampled its 31-customer subset from this exact 342-customer pool with np.random.default_rng(42). The same seed reproduces exactly which real customers that study used, so their archetype labels, from the 342-customer clustering above, and their real simulated risk can be compared directly, not approximated.

There is a solid physical reason to expect this connection, not just wishful thinking. An archetype describes a customer’s own demand shape. Voltage rise from PV depends on how much power flows backward through the line at the moment PV output is highest, usually midday. A customer who already draws very little at midday leaves more room for that export before their own local voltage rises too far. Thermal loading from EV charging depends on how much extra power lands on the transformer at the moment it is already busiest, usually the evening peak. A customer whose archetype already peaks hard in the evening adds an EV’s charging load right on top of their own peak, not into a quiet period. If archetype carried no real information, none of this would show up in the simulation below. If it does, archetype becomes a genuine planning signal, not just a label.

Two functions do the work. build_ausnet_network() rebuilds the same feeder Chapters 1 and 2 already solved. per_customer_pv_voltage_risk() sweeps a PV scenario across it and returns each customer’s own worst-case bus voltage, not only the feeder-wide maximum Chapter 2 reported. A matching per_customer_ev_thermal_contribution() sweeps an EV scenario and returns each customer’s own peak kW draw at the feeder’s own peak-loading instant.

Both scenarios use exactly the same seed and the same sampling call as Chapter 2’s own study, so they draw the same 31 real customers.

archetype vmax_pu_mean vmax_pu_count peak_kw_contribution_mean peak_kw_contribution_count
0 1.098 7 8.4303 7
1 1.097 5 9.8764 5
2 1.0994 10 10.6108 10
3 1.1019 4 8.378 4
4 1.0956 5 8.0516 5

Archetype 3 carries the highest mean voltage risk (1.1019 per-unit) but ranks only fourth of five on mean thermal contribution (8.38 kW). Archetype 2 carries the highest mean thermal contribution (10.61 kW) but ranks only second on voltage risk (1.0994 per-unit). Different customer groups lead different risks on the same feeder, mirroring Chapter 2’s own PV-versus-EV finding, now visible at the archetype level.

Key Concept

Archetype membership carries a real signal, and it does not point the same way for both DER types. A DSO that only checks “which archetype is risky” without asking “risky for which constraint” would still miss half the picture. The sample is small, 31 customers across five archetypes, so this is a real, checkable association worth pairing with more data before treating it as settled, not a large-scale statistical claim.

A controlled test: can clustering discover a new behavior?

Every check so far describes behavior already sitting in the data. A DSO’s real question is forward-looking: if a specific group of customers started charging an EV tomorrow, would re- running this same clustering actually notice? That is a testable claim, not a hypothetical one. Take a real EV charging profile, inject it onto a known set of real households, and check whether unsupervised clustering rediscovers that group on its own, with no label telling it where to look.

real EV charging shape: peak 1.49 kW/EV at half-hour 42
40 real households picked at random, spread across every archetype above:
archetype count
0 5
1 15
2 10
3 6
4 4

Add the real EV charging shape directly onto each adopter’s own real kW draw, before normalizing, then re-cluster everyone. Two versions: k=5, the same number of archetypes used throughout this chapter, and k=6, one extra slot in case the adopters form a genuinely new shape rather than just relabeling an existing one.

k dominant_cluster recall purity
5 0 0.9 0.379
6 4 0.975 0.513

The bar chart shows where the adopters landed. It does not show what that cluster’s shape actually looks like, or whether it resembles a real EV charging bump rather than an existing archetype’s own evening peak.

Key Concept

Recall is strong: 90% of the injected households land in the same single cluster at k=5, and 97.5% at k=6. Purity is honest, not perfect: that cluster is only 38-51% actual adopters, the rest are real households whose own baseline behavior already looks similar to an evening-charging EV shape. Re-clustering does not perfectly separate every new adopter from a household that already resembled one, but it reliably flags where the new behavior concentrates. A DSO re-running this analysis periodically would see this cluster grow and know something real changed there, even without perfectly identifying every individual adopter.

Sensitivity: how much history does one clustering run need?

The stability check above looks at whether an archetype remains consistent across different situations. But there is another, more practical question to consider. Within a single season, how much data does one clustering run actually need to be reliable?

This is important for a DSO deciding whether to run this analysis on just the first week of readings from a new meter instead of waiting for a full quarter.

They need to know this before considering the stability question. Chapter 3 looked at a similar question about how much data and how many meters are needed for phase identification. This applies the same approach here.

window ARI vs season baseline Silhouette
1 day 0.019 0.088
1 week 0.113 0.09
1 month (30 days) 0.345 0.171
1 season (90 days) 1.0 0.163

A single day of data hardly matches the season-long baseline. The ARI for one day is 0.02, which is almost random. Even after a week, the score is still low at 0.11. After a month, the score improves to 0.35, better, but still not stable. Choosing a season-long window was not a random decision at the start of this analysis. It marks the point where the archetype labels stop changing on their own, before the cross-quarter stability question above even gets asked. A DSO trying to shortcut this analysis with a single day or week of new meter data would be clustering noise, not behavior.

Does this generalize beyond AusNet?

Every check so far asks whether an archetype is stable, across quarters, across random seeds, across how much history feeds the clustering run. None of them can ask whether an archetype is right, because AusNet’s own 342 real customers carry no independent, ground-truth label for what kind of customer each one actually is. That is not a gap this chapter can close with more AusNet data, no matter how much of it: the label simply does not exist for these real, anonymous meters.

NREL’s real ResStock and ComStock building-stock data, 760 real buildings stratified across 19 real subtypes from single-family homes to hospitals, does carry a genuine ground-truth label: building_type, assigned independently of anything this chapter’s own clustering could see. Reusing the exact same recipe this chapter already committed to, peak-normalized mean daily shape, KMeans, no new method invented for this check, on a population where the right answer is actually known makes a real question checkable for the first time: does clustering by shape alone recover real, known categories, or does it find something else?

Key Concept

Two indices compare a discovered partition against a known one, both correcting for the agreement chance alone would produce. Hubert and Arabie’s Adjusted Rand Index counts every pair of objects and asks whether the two partitions agree on whether that pair belongs together, then subtracts the agreement expected from random chance and rescales so a perfect match scores 1 and a chance-level match scores 0 [9]: \[\text{ARI} = \frac{\sum_{ij}\binom{n_{ij}}{2} - \left[\sum_i \binom{a_i}{2}\sum_j \binom{b_j}{2}\right]\big/\binom{n}{2}}{\frac{1}{2}\left[\sum_i \binom{a_i}{2} + \sum_j \binom{b_j}{2}\right] - \left[\sum_i \binom{a_i}{2}\sum_j \binom{b_j}{2}\right]\big/\binom{n}{2}}\] where \(n_{ij}\) is the number of objects placed in true group \(i\) and discovered group \(j\) at once, and \(a_i\), \(b_j\) are each partition’s own group sizes. Strehl and Ghosh’s Normalized Mutual Information asks a different question, how much does knowing one partition reduce real uncertainty about the other [10]: \[\text{NMI}(U, V) = \frac{I(U, V)}{\tfrac{1}{2}\left[H(U) + H(V)\right]}\] where \(I(U,V)\) is the mutual information between the two partitions and \(H(\cdot)\) is Shannon entropy. Neither index rewards a discovered partition just for having a similar number of groups to the true one; both only reward it for actually grouping the same real objects together.

NREL shape matrix: (760, 48) (buildings, half-hours), 19 real subtypes
k ARI NMI
5 0.0634 0.1872
10 0.0915 0.2394
19 0.0781 0.2683

Every real ARI is close to 0, and every real NMI stays well short of 1, at every k tried, including k=19, matching the true number of subtypes exactly. Shape-based clustering, the same method that gave AusNet’s own five archetypes real, checkable value earlier in this chapter, does not recover NREL’s real building-type labels here. This is not the same question as before: AusNet’s 342 customers are homes and homes only, so real archetypes there reflect genuine behavioral diversity within one functional category. NREL’s 760 buildings span single-family homes, hospitals, warehouses, and schools at once, categories that plausibly differ in more than daily shape alone, and shape-based clustering alone is not enough to cleanly separate them.

Cluster Buildings Residential Commercial
0 133 33 100
1 190 6 184
2 107 78 29
3 176 70 106
4 154 13 141

The clusters are not a random shuffle either: one cluster comes out almost entirely commercial, another skews residential, a real, partial structure the low ARI/NMI scores do not fully credit on their own, since those indices score agreement with the full 19-way (or 5-way) label, not the coarser residential/commercial split. The honest reading holds either way: shape alone captures some real structure in a population this diverse, a known building type, occupancy schedule, or floor area would plausibly sharpen it further, and this chapter’s own AusNet archetypes should be read as behavioral archetypes within one real customer category, not a general-purpose recipe for discovering functional building categories from shape alone.

A table of ARI and NMI numbers states that finding; it does not let a reader see it. Projecting the same 48-dimensional shape embedding this chapter has used throughout down to its first two principal components, the same reduction this book’s own ark.plot.clustering.cluster_scatter already applies everywhere else a shape embedding needs a 2D picture, turns the abstract “does not recover” verdict into something checkable by eye.

Side by side, the two panels show exactly what the ARI/NMI numbers already reported: the five discovered clusters cut across this projection in bands that only loosely track the real residential/commercial boundary. Some discovered clusters sit almost entirely on one side of that boundary, echoing the composition table above; others straddle it, buildings whose daily shape looks similar enough to land in the same cluster despite belonging to genuinely different real categories. No amount of staring at this projection would let a reader guess the true label perfectly from the discovered one, the same limit the ARI of roughly 0.06 to 0.09 already quantified.

The cluster means themselves are the more concrete artifact a DSO would actually use, the same member-profiles-and-mean chart this chapter has shown for AusNet’s own archetypes throughout, now run on NREL’s real, more diverse population.

These five discovered shapes read as genuinely distinct real behaviors, and the composition table above lines up with them directly. Cluster 1, 97% commercial, shows a sharp, tall midday peak, roughly 10:00 to 14:00, the shape of a business open standard daytime hours. Cluster 4, 92% commercial, peaks narrower still, later in the afternoon. Cluster 0, three-quarters commercial, stays relatively flat and elevated across the entire day, plausible for a round-the-clock operation rather than a nine-to-five one. Cluster 2, the most residential-leaning group at 73%, is the clear outlier of the five: a broader, gentler rise into the afternoon and evening, closer to a home’s own daily rhythm than any of the sharp commercial peaks. Cluster 3, the most genuinely mixed group, 40% residential, reads as a broad, low-contrast plateau, no single real behavior dominating clearly, exactly what a 60/40 mix of two different populations sharing one cluster should look like. That is a genuinely useful, real finding on its own: shape-based clustering is not producing noise, it recovers real, interpretable behavioral groups, largely tracking the coarse residential/commercial split even where it does not track the full 19-way label. What it does not reliably do, and what the ARI/NMI numbers above already quantified, is line those groups up with NREL’s own independently assigned building-type labels one-to-one. A cluster that looks “sharp midday peak” plausibly contains a real mix of offices, retail spaces, and schools that all happen to draw power on the same daytime schedule for different underlying reasons, a distinction daily shape alone cannot resolve, whatever a known building type, occupancy schedule, or floor area might resolve instead.

Feeder-level clustering

Everything above groups customers. Ledva and Mathieu and Li et al. ask the same question one level up: do whole feeders fall into a handful of aggregate archetypes too, residential-heavy, commercial-heavy, mixed, useful for a DSO planning investment across many feeders at once, not just one at a time [11], [12]. AusNet’s own single 31-customer feeder cannot answer this, there is only one of it. This section reuses SMART-DS, National Renewable Energy Laboratory (NREL)’s real, validated synthetic distribution testbed for the Austin, TX region, 33 real Low Voltage (LV) feeders across 8 substations, each with its own real per-building demand profiles.

Each feeder’s network model uses 200 to 350 real customer profiles from a shared, region-wide pool. Fetching every profile for all 33 feeders would mean thousands of small downloads, even though this section only needs each feeder’s overall load shape, not every individual customer. Instead, the fetch script samples a real, kW-stratified subset per feeder from the same shared pool:

uv run python scripts/fetch_part4_smartds_data.py

Two small helpers do the work: feeder_load_profiles() parses a feeder’s own Loads.dss into (customer name, kW, profile id) triples, and feeder_aggregate_shape() sums whichever sampled profile CSVs actually landed on disk into one real, kW-weighted aggregate demand curve per feeder.

Since these are common, real patterns found throughout the region, and not unique to each customer, even a small sample from each feeder represents a much larger part of that feeder’s customer base than the sample size alone suggests: samples drawn for other feeders often include patterns this feeder’s own customers also happen to use.

33 feeders with a usable real aggregate shape
statistic value
count 33.0
mean 769.1818181818181
std 640.6141806181713
min 1.0
25% 125.0
50% 767.0
75% 1126.0
max 2580.0

Coverage varies. Some feeders have thin coverage, only a handful of real customers whose sampled shape happened to match, worth keeping in mind: their own cluster membership rests on less real evidence than a feeder with hundreds of matched customers (median 767, ranging 1 to 2,580), the same “how many meters do you need” caution Chapter 3’s own sensitivity section raised for customer-level phase identification.

k inertia silhouette davies_bouldin
2 4.906 0.668 0.436
3 2.813 0.576 0.508
4 1.982 0.442 0.839
5 1.608 0.423 0.85
6 1.292 0.44 0.745

Clustering at the feeder level gives much clearer separation than at the customer level: a silhouette score of 0.67 at k=2, against 0.23 for individual customers. This is expected, not a contradiction. Averaging at the feeder level smooths out the individual noise that caused overlap at the customer level in the first place. Unlike the customer-level case, k=2 actually is the validity-index optimum here, not a compromise past it.

Two distinct feeder archetypes emerge. The first shows a sharp evening peak and much lower daytime demand, consistent with a residential-heavy mix. The second holds a sustained plateau through the middle of the day before a noisier, more irregular evening, consistent with a commercial- heavy mix whose demand does not track the residential dinner-and-bedtime rhythm the rest of this chapter has been clustering on. Exactly the “different customer mix” a DSO would want to plan for differently, now visible at the feeder level, not just guessed at.

Why bother

Chapter 1 named three ways DER strains a feeder: fluctuation, load growth, and constraint violations. Every study since Chapter 2 has answered “how much strain, and where” one feeder at a time, after the fact. This chapter’s contribution is different: knowing which customers are likely to cause which kind of strain, before they ever apply to connect solar or an EV charger.

On the utility side, the archetype-versus-risk cross-tabulation above is a leading indicator, not a retrospective one. Archetype 3 flags voltage risk; archetype 2 flags thermal risk; neither flag would exist without clustering customers by behavior first. A DSO triaging a connection queue can prioritize monitoring and reinforcement spend on the archetypes this chapter actually flags as high-risk, and pair the right lever from Chapter 2, Volt-Watt, Volt-VAr, or storage, with the right archetype at connection time, instead of a one-size-fits- all rule applied after a hosting-capacity study finds a problem. The controlled EV injection test adds a second, forward-looking piece: re-clustering periodically would catch a new adopter cluster forming with 90-97.5% recall, a real early-warning signal for where DER adoption is concentrating, not just where it already has.

On the customer side, the same cross-tabulation cuts the other way. A customer whose archetype this chapter shows as low-risk can get a faster, cheaper connection approval. One whose archetype shows real risk can be paired with the right mitigation up front, storage bundled with the connection, say, instead of a surprise curtailment discovered months later. A tariff targeted by real behavioral archetype, not a blanket residential rate, is a fairer cost-to-serve match for both sides of the meter.

The stability finding closes the loop. This two-sided value only holds if an archetype-based decision stays valid after it is made. The conformal confidence sets above already flag which customers’ archetype labels are solid and which are ambiguous at the moment of decision. The cross-quarter check goes further: it shows the label itself can drift within a year, 0.06-0.34 agreement against 0.96-0.99 for pure algorithmic noise. A stable archetype is a trustworthy basis for a connection- approval or tariff decision that persists. A churning one means last year’s archetype is targeting the wrong households today, a real, costed risk, repeated re-clustering, or decisions quietly going stale, a DSO would actually have to weigh, not a hypothetical one.

Where this leaves Part 4

Five real customer archetypes, tested against three separate validity indices rather than assumed. A deep-clustering contender that honestly loses to a much simpler method on this data. A conformal confidence set that turns “some archetypes overlap” into a checkable, per-customer number. A stability check none of this chapter’s ten reference papers run, showing those archetypes are a real but genuinely time-varying trait, not a fixed label. A sensitivity check showing why a full season, not a day or a week, earns that trust. A controlled test showing clustering can rediscover a real behavior change with 90-97.5% recall. And a direct, checkable link from archetype to which real DER constraint, voltage or thermal, a customer’s own future PV or EV adoption is likely to trigger. Two real feeder archetypes on top of that, the same method one level up.

These archetypes are not the end of the story. They are a grouping key the rest of Part 4 reuses. Thread 3 builds anomaly-detection baselines per archetype rather than one baseline for all 342 customers pooled together. Thread 4 applies DER-growth scenarios per archetype rather than uniformly across a feeder, using exactly the enrichment found above to decide where growth is likely to bind first.
